questiontext.text: Area of trapezium with parallel sides \({a},{b}\) and height \({c}\):

name.text: Chapter 1 — Unit I — Sylow theorems, solvable and nilpotent groups

Chapter 1 — Unit I — Sylow theorems, solvable and nilpotent groups

generalfeedback.text: \(\sqrt 2\) cannot be expressed as \(p/q\) with integers.

questiontext.text: For the topic Chapter 1 — Unit I — Sylow theorems, solvable and nilpotent groups, compute \( {a}^{2}+{b}^{2} \).

name.text: Chapter 1 — Unit I — Sylow theorems, solvable and nilpotent groups

questiontext.text: Remainder theorem: when \(p(x)\) is divided by \((x-{a})\), the remainder is:

name.text: Chapter 1 — Unit I — Sylow theorems, solvable and nilpotent groups

questiontext.text: A pair of linear equations represents coincident lines iff:

name.text: Chapter 1 — Unit I — Sylow theorems, solvable and nilpotent groups
Chapter 1 — Unit I — Sylow theorems, solvable and nilpotent groups
Chapter 1 — Unit I — Sylow theorems, solvable and nilpotent groups

questiontext.text: For lines \({a}x+{b}y={c}\) and \({=2*{a}}x+{=2*{b}}y={=2*{c}}\), the system has:

generalfeedback.text: Second equation is double the first \(\Rightarrow\) coincident lines \(\Rightarrow\) infinite solutions.

name.text: Chapter 1 — Unit I — Sylow theorems, solvable and nilpotent groups

Chapter 1 — Unit I — Sylow theorems, solvable and nilpotent groups

questiontext.text: A ring satisfies (in addition to additive group structure):

name.text: Chapter 1 — Unit I — Sylow theorems, solvable and nilpotent groups

questiontext.text: In a group, the inverse of each element is:

name.text: Chapter 1 — Unit I — Sylow theorems, solvable and nilpotent groups
Chapter 1 — Unit I — Sylow theorems, solvable and nilpotent groups
Chapter 1 — Unit I — Sylow theorems, solvable and nilpotent groups

questiontext.text: For lines \({a}x+{b}y={c}\) and \({a}x+{b}y={d}\) with \({c}\ne{d}\), the system has:

generalfeedback.text: Same coefficients, different RHS \(\Rightarrow\) parallel non-coincident lines \(\Rightarrow\) no solution.

name.text: Chapter 1 — Unit I — Sylow theorems, solvable and nilpotent groups
